question: Define the classes P and NP. What is the fundamental question regarding the relationship between these two classes?
expectedPoints: Class P consists of decision problems that can be solved by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
Class NP consists of decision problems for which a proposed solution (certificate) can be verified by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
The fundamental P vs NP question asks whether every problem in NP is also in P, i.e., whether P = NP.

question: Explain the concept of NP-completeness. Why is the Cook-Levin theorem considered the foundation of this concept?
expectedPoints: A problem is NP-complete if it is in NP and every problem in NP is polynomial-time reducible to it.
The Cook-Levin theorem proved that the Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT) is NP-complete.
This theorem is foundational because it provided the first example of an NP-complete problem, allowing other problems to be proven NP-complete by showing a polynomial-time reduction from SAT.

question: What does it mean for a problem to be decidable? Provide an example of an undecidable problem.
expectedPoints: A problem is decidable if there exists a Turing machine (algorithm) that, for any input, will always halt and produce the correct 'yes' or 'no' answer.
An example of an undecidable problem is the Halting Problem.
The Halting Problem asks whether a given Turing machine will halt on a given input, and it was proven by Alan Turing that no general algorithm exists to solve it for all possible machine-input pairs.
